name: Dark Triad (Machiavellianism, Narcissism, Psychopathy)
core_claim: The Dark Triad is a cluster of three socially aversive but subclinical personality traits: Machiavellianism (strategic manipulation, cynical worldview, instrumental view of others), narcissism (grandiosity, entitlement, dominance-seeking), and psychopathy (callous affect, impulsivity, low empathy, thrill-seeking). The traits share a common 'dark core' of callous self-interest and a willingness to instrumentalise others. For media preference, the theoretical link is affective/moral congruence and disposition theory: enjoyment of fiction is partly gated by moral alignment with characters, so audiences whose own moral outlook overlaps with a villain's or anti-hero's are freed from the usual moral disapproval and can engage positively (enjoyment, identification, fascination, empathy) with morally dark protagonists. Kjeldgaard-Christiansen et al. frame this as 'villain positivity': people high in dark traits share enough of the villain's immoral outlook that transgressive characters become appealing rather than repellent. The construct therefore plausibly predicts appetite for the moral-complexity end of the theme vocabulary (anti-heroes, villain-as-protagonist, moral ambiguity, vengeance, power-corrupts narratives) rather than any single genre.
predictive_validity_for_media: Direct evidence is real but modest and concentrated in a few studies. (1) Kjeldgaard-Christiansen, Fiskaali, Hogh-Olesen & Johnson (2021, Poetics, 'Do dark personalities prefer dark characters?') found that individual differences in dark personality traits significantly predicted multiple forms of villain positivity — enjoyment, identification, fascination, and empathy toward fictional villains — supporting moral-congruence accounts. (2) Greenwood et al. (2020, summarised in Psychology Today) on anti-heroes: Machiavellianism (and trait aggression) was linked specifically to enjoyment of anti-heroes motivated by revenge or personal gain, while psychopathy/aggression best predicted perceived similarity to the anti-hero; narcissism was 'much less important' across outcomes. (3) Greenwood & colleagues / comic-book work found Dark Triad traits and hero/villain status jointly predicted parasocial relationships with characters. (4) Jonason et al. ('The Antihero in Popular Culture', Life History Theory framing) tie a fast-life-history strategy to anti-hero appeal. Caveats critical for HelpME2C: effect sizes are generally small-to-moderate, the literature predicts THEMATIC/character preference (dark protagonists, revenge motives, moral transgression) and PARASOCIAL engagement, not genre per se, and there is essentially no evidence on completion rate or cross-medium (TV-from-personality) prediction. Machiavellianism is the most consistently useful subtrait for media-preference signal; narcissism is the weakest. [uncertain]
instrument_design: Two dominant short instruments. (1) Short Dark Triad (SD3; Jones & Paulhus, 2014): 27 items, 9 per trait, 5-point Likert, ~5-7 min; the psychometric gold standard among brief measures, with stronger convergent/incremental validity than the Dirty Dozen. (2) Dark Triad Dirty Dozen (DTDD; Jonason & Webster, 2010): 12 items, 4 per trait, completes in ~1-2 min, very high internal consistency but criticised for content overlap, thin coverage, and weaker convergent validity (its narcissism items capture both grandiose and vulnerable facets, where SD3 captures mainly grandiose). For a sub-2-minute, 6-10-item onboarding the only viable option is the DTDD (12 items) or a further-reduced 'dirtier dozen' style 6-9-item screen — but any reduction below the DTDD sacrifices the already-questioned reliability further. A minimum viable version would be ~2 items per trait (6 items) framed behaviourally, which would yield a coarse single 'dark-core' score rather than reliable subtrait scores. Honest assessment: there is no validated <2-minute Dark Triad instrument that produces trustworthy subtrait scores; the floor for defensible measurement is the 12-item DTDD. [uncertain]
cold_start_applicability: Moderate-to-low as a standalone cold-start signal, and narrower than broad-bandwidth instruments. Because it is collected via self-report at onboarding, it does provide signal with zero viewing history — its entire value is pre-interaction. But three problems limit it for cold start: (1) it is a narrow construct that informs only the moral-complexity / dark-protagonist axis of preference, not breadth of taste, so it cannot anchor a full taste profile alone; (2) the documented effect sizes are small, so it shifts priors weakly; (3) faking and social-desirability pressure are acute for these specifically aversive items at first contact (see quiz UX), degrading signal quality exactly when there is no behavioural data to correct it. There is no published evidence it predicts TV preference cross-medium from personality alone. Best understood as a single tilt parameter ('comfort with / appetite for morally dark narratives') layered on top of a broader instrument (e.g. Big Five Openness + sensation-seeking), not as a primary cold-start engine.
group_compatibility_use: Reasonable theoretical fit for the moral-darkness axis but no joint-media-satisfaction evidence. The relevant literature is dyadic-relationship, not co-viewing: assortative-mating studies (e.g. ScienceDirect 2024, UK/Fiji + meta-analysis) find significantly positive partner similarity for psychopathy, marginally positive for Machiavellianism, slightly negative for narcissism — i.e. people do somewhat pair with similar partners on the darker traits. Kardum et al. (dyadic response-surface and APIM studies) find Dark Triad traits exert mainly NEGATIVE actor and partner effects on relationship satisfaction, with (dis)similarity effects for psychopathy and narcissism — notably, high-psychopathy-paired-with-low is especially dissatisfying. The transferable implication for two-person recommendation: for the dark-narrative tilt, INTERSECTION / least-misery aggregation is the safer default — recommend morally dark content only when BOTH partners score above threshold, because a low-dark viewer paired with a high-dark recommendation risks active aversion (the asymmetric-dissimilarity cost mirrors the satisfaction findings). Simple averaging is risky here precisely because dissimilarity is costly. There is NO direct evidence that any aggregation strategy predicts joint VIEWING satisfaction; the dyadic findings are about relationship quality, not co-watching. Treat as: gate dark content behind a both-partners-comfortable check, lean conservative. [uncertain]
quiz_ux_implications: High-risk for direct elicitation. These are among the most socially undesirable items in personality assessment ('I tend to manipulate others to get my way', 'I tend to lack remorse'), so social-desirability bias and deliberate faking-good are severe — far worse than for Big Five or values items. Acquiescence bias also applies (DTDD/SD3 are keyed mostly in one direction). Direct 'rate how much you agree' framing will systematically under-report dark traits and compress variance. Mitigations: (a) NEVER label or frame the quiz as measuring 'dark' traits; (b) prefer indirect, content-anchored elicitation — ask about reactions to characters and scenarios ('How satisfying is it when a character takes revenge?', 'Do you root for the villain?') which doubles as moral-darkness preference signal AND is far less threatening than trait self-attribution; (c) forced-choice or scenario formats reduce desirability pull versus agree/disagree Likert. For HelpME2C the strong recommendation is to elicit the PREFERENCE (appetite for dark narratives) directly via character/scenario items and skip self-report of the trait itself — the preference is what we actually want and is both more honest and less creepy.
cross_cultural_validity: Weak and contested — a real liability for a multilingual catalog. The SD3 has repeatedly failed strict cross-cultural invariance: across 18 cultural/national contexts it failed even configural invariance, achieving metric invariance only with item parcels; a 3-society Asian study (South Korea, China, Philippines, n=443) found that except for Machiavellianism, models failed configural invariance, though men scored higher everywhere. A 2025 network-based (exploratory graph analysis) study across 14 cultures was more optimistic, supporting metric invariance via a network approach and identifying both universal and culture-specific indicators. Culture accounts for ~6-16% of variance in Dark Triad scores. Validated translations exist (SD3 Chinese SD3-C, Serbian, Polish DTDD). Bottom line: trait-level comparisons across HelpME2C's Korean/Japanese/Chinese/Arabic/Hindi user base are NOT safely comparable, and meaning of items (especially manipulation and remorse) shifts with cultural norms around assertiveness and collectivism. This reinforces the recommendation to elicit content PREFERENCE rather than trait scores — preference for revenge/villain narratives is more directly comparable across cultures than self-reported manipulativeness.
privacy_consent_implications: High sensitivity. Dark Triad scores are explicit psychological profiling and, under GDPR, inferences that could relate to health (psychopathy borders clinical antisociality), beliefs, or other Article 9 categories may constitute special-category data when the inference is intentional and acted upon — which it would be here (we draw the inference and treat the user differently based on it). ICO guidance: intentional inference of special-category-adjacent attributes triggers Article 9, requiring BOTH an Article 6 lawful basis AND a separate Article 9 condition (realistically explicit, specific, informed consent — generic 'improve our service' consent is invalid). Concrete risks: (1) re-identification and the stigma of a stored 'high psychopathy' label; (2) sensitive-inference creep (linking dark-trait scores to other behaviour); (3) reputational/data-breach harm given how pejorative the labels are. Recommended posture for HelpME2C: do NOT store raw Dark Triad trait scores. If used at all, collect only the derived, neutral preference parameter ('dark-narrative comfort level'), use it transiently to seed recommendations, and avoid persisting any pejorative trait label. If trait-level data is ever stored, it requires explicit Article 9 consent and an ADR. Given ADR-0012's privacy posture this construct is a stop-and-ask candidate. [uncertain]
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Maps tightly to the moral-darkness / power axis and is largely orthogonal to relationship and texture dimensions. High dark-core (esp. Machiavellianism + psychopathy) predicts preference for: moral-compromise, vigilante-justice, complicity-with-evil, vengeance-and-its-cost, power-corrupts, corruption-and-cover-up, rise-and-fall, satire-of-power, dark-comedy, and (via villain-as-protagonist appeal) toxic-relationship and betrayal-and-forgiveness when framed from the transgressor's side. Psychopathy specifically (thrill/impulsivity facet) adds chase-and-escape, survival-and-endurance, single-incident-fallout, and existential/cosmic-horror appetite (callous affect tolerates bleakness). Machiavellianism specifically adds heist-and-caper, institutional-failure, propaganda-and-truth, and class-conflict (strategic/power-game narratives). Narcissism (weakest predictor) loosely tilts toward celebrity-and-fame, alter-ego, and rise-and-fall (grandiosity/status themes). LOW dark-core predicts AVERSION to villain-protagonist content and a pull toward redemption-arc, guilt-and-atonement, found-family, and morally legible heroism. The cleanest single derived dimension is a 'moral-complexity comfort' slider gating the Moral cluster and rise-and-fall / power-corrupts.
implementation_recommendation: PARTIAL — include the PREFERENCE, exclude the trait instrument. Do NOT administer the SD3 or DTDD as a self-report trait quiz: faking/social-desirability bias is severe, cross-cultural invariance is poor, the privacy/Article-9 burden is high, and effect sizes on media preference are only small-to-moderate. Instead, capture the construct's useful output — appetite for morally dark narratives — via 2-3 content-anchored, scenario/character preference items embedded in the broader onboarding quiz (e.g. 'How much do you enjoy stories told from the villain's point of view?', 'A character takes brutal revenge and gets away with it — satisfying or uncomfortable?', 'Do you prefer clear heroes, or morally grey protagonists?'). These items: (a) directly produce the 'moral-complexity comfort' slider we want; (b) sidestep storing pejorative trait labels; (c) travel across cultures better than manipulation/remorse self-reports; (d) feel like a taste quiz, not a clinical screen. Dependency: most valuable when combined with Openness/sensation-seeking elicitation (which carry the breadth of taste this narrow construct cannot). Aggregation for two-person mode: gate dark content behind a both-comfortable (intersection / least-misery) check, never simple averaging. Revisit administering the actual instrument only if (i) we gain robust behavioural data to correct faking and (ii) a validated <2-min cross-culturally invariant short form appears.

Jones & Paulhus (2014) 'Introducing the Short Dark Triad (SD3)' Assessment — https://www2.psych.ubc.ca/~dpaulhus/research/DARK_TRAITS/ARTICLES/ASSESST.2014.with.Jones.pdf (free). Maples et al. 'A Test of Two Brief Measures: Dirty Dozen and SD3' — https://www.researchgate.net/publication/258920738 (paywalled). DTDD overview — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_Triad_Dirty_Dozen (free). Anti-hero appeal summary (Greenwood et al.) — https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/unique-everybody-else/202012/why-do-antiheroes-appeal-people-dark-traits (free). 'The Antihero in Popular Culture: Life History Theory and the Dark Triad' — https://www.researchgate.net/publication/232590024 (free PDF). Cross-cultural: SD3 across 14 cultures (2025) — https://www.scl.rs/papers/ATomasevic2025_PersIndividDiffer246.pdf (free); SD3 in three Asian societies — https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/ajsp.12378 (paywalled); SD3-Chinese — https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Delroy-Paulhus/publication/333097914 (free). Dyadic: Kardum et al. 'Dark triad traits and relationship satisfaction' J Personality (2024) — https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/jopy.12857; assortative mating UK/Fiji + meta-analysis — https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191886924002149 (paywalled). Privacy: ICO special category data guidance — https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/lawful-basis/a-guide-to-lawful-basis/special-category-data/ (free).
